denial_pattern_sources: plan_exclusion_overrides_state_mandate: NCSL State Health Insurance Mandates Database 2024; documented in CA, NJ, IL enforcement actions
wrong_appeals_contact: State Insurance Commissioner complaint logs (CA, NY, TX) 2023
denial_letter_text:
Dear Plan Member,

We have completed our review of the request for Additional outpatient psychotherapy visits related to Major depressive disorder, recurrent (F33.1).

After clinical review using Cigna Medical Coverage Policies and MCG Care Guidelines, we are unable to approve coverage at this time.

Determination summary: Annual 20-visit behavioral health cap exceeded. Our records were reviewed against the applicable utilization-management criteria for prior authorization determinations. Specifically, this review evaluated whether the requested service meets the plan's definition of medical necessity and whether required administrative steps were completed. Annual visit cap (PT, chiro, mental health) exceeded.

Clinical review notes indicate the documentation submitted does not satisfy the criteria required to authorize Additional outpatient psychotherapy visits. If you believe additional information was not considered, you may request a peer-to-peer discussion with a medical director; however, any such discussion must occur within the timeframe stated in your plan materials.

You have the right to appeal this determination. To file a standard appeal, submit a written request with supporting medical records within 180 days of the date of this letter. Include your member identification, provider information, and any letters of medical necessity.

Sincerely,

Cigna Health Care Management
clinical_context:
The 32-year-old member carries a documented diagnosis of Major depressive disorder, recurrent (F33.1). He has been under continuous care with treating specialists who recommended Additional outpatient psychotherapy visits as the next appropriate step.

Parity: no analogous cap on cardiology follow-ups. Treating clinicians submitted office notes, medication trials with dates and doses, and objective findings supporting medical necessity. The insurer's letter instead relies on visit limit exceeded without engaging the specific evidence already on file.

Contrary to the denial narrative, the chart shows prior therapies were attempted for adequate duration and failed or were contraindicated. The appeal should emphasize documented step-therapy exceptions where applicable, correct application of specialty-appropriate guidelines, and any procedural defects in the notice (for example vague policy edition citations or incomplete appeal rights). The member remains symptomatic and faces delay-related harm if the requested service is further postponed.
